notes.note: In late 54 BC, amid electoral chaos and violence, there was talk of making Pompey dictator to restore order. The rumour frightened the senatorial class, who saw it as a threat to constitutional government; it helped sway the jurors to acquit Gabinius.

The repetundae charge: misconduct and extortion by a provincial governor, tried before a standing court. Gabinius faced it for his governorship of Syria, and Cicero predicts (rightly) that he will be convicted on it.

notes.anchor: Pacideianus matched against Aeserninus the Samnite
notes.note: A pair of gladiators made proverbial by a vivid passage of the satirist Lucilius. Cicero uses the image to say that prosecuting Gabinius would have meant a savage, mutually wounding duel with Pompey, who backed him.

notes.anchor: the verses you want me to write for you
notes.note: The brothers exchanged verse compositions; Cicero pleads lack of both leisure and inspiration (Greek enthousiasmos), citing his anxiety about the coming political year. He gracefully cedes the palm in such writing to Quintus.

notes.anchor: I will charge Chrysippus and speak with Tyrannio
notes.note: Tyrannio of Amisus, the Greek grammarian who arranged Cicero's library, and Chrysippus, a learned freedman, were Cicero's go-to men for acquiring, copying, and exchanging books. Good copies, Cicero notes, could not simply be bought.

notes.anchor: I want to be present for Pomptinus's triumph
notes.note: C. Pomptinus, praetor 63 and governor of Transalpine Gaul, had long sought a triumph for his victory over the Allobroges. Tribunes and praetors disputed its legality, so it was finally staged in 54 BC under cover of an early-morning vote and the consul Appius's backing.
